article_items.title: Amazon will show AI product images when you search for some reason | TechCrunch

More from TechCrunch Amazon will show AI product images when you search for some reason In what may be one of the more questionable uses of AI to date, Amazon announced on Wednesday that it will display AI-generated.
Amazon will use visual search and AI to show AI-generated product images that match your search queries.
The idea is that clicking one would direct you to search results that better match that style, powered by Amazon’s visual search capabilities.
The retailer says it will help guide users to products.

Microsoft launches Scout, an OpenClaw-inspired personal assistant ‘What a joke’: GitHub Copilot’s new token-based billing spurs consternation among devs I put Google’s 24/7 AI assistant Gemini Spark to work, and it’s.
For instance, if you search for a blue gingham dress, you might see a few dress styles - short or long sleeves, varying lengths, and other differences - appear as visual options.
On the more useful end, Amazon already summarizes customer reviews via AI, so you don’t have to read them all to get a sense of the key pros and cons of a product.
Here’s how Amazon says in a blog post that the feature will work.
The feature follows a number of other attempts by Amazon to integrate AI into its retail site and shopping app, with mixed results.

And there’s the fairly obvious question of why you’d make up product images when you have a website full of real photographs of real products - which is presumably what an online shopper actually wants to see.
In reality, it’s somewhat bananas for a retailer to make up fake products as a way of guiding users to search results.

article_items.title: Fundamental’s Large Tabular Model NEXUS is now available on Amazon SageMaker JumpStart | Amazon Web Services
article_items.description: In this post, we show you how to get started with NEXUS on Amazon SageMaker JumpStart, walk through the deployment process, and demonstrate how to run predictions against your enterprise datasets.
article_items.excerpt: In this post, we show you how to get started with NEXUS on Amazon SageMaker JumpStart, walk through the deployment process, and demonstrate how to run predictions against your enterprise datasets.
article_items.summary: Artificial Intelligence Fundamental’s Large Tabular Model NEXUS is now available on Amazon SageMaker JumpStart Today, we’re announcing support for Fundamental’s NEXUS model on Amazon SageMaker AI . With this launch, you can deploy a foundation model (FM) purpose-built for tabular data prediction. NEXUS is architected for tabular data and provides advantages such as the following: Permutation invariance – Recognizes that changing column order doesn’t change meaning, which differs from how transformers handle data. Why existing approaches fall short The most valuable enterprise data sits in tables such as spreadsheets, enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems, customer relationship management (CRM) systems, and. Non-sequential reasoning – Most AI models predict sequential data (for example, the next word or the next pixel). NEXUS is purpose-built for this data and helps you go from raw structured data to production-grade predictions without extensive feature engineering or model training.

article_items.title: Festo launches lightweight pneumatic gripper and tests GripperAI - The Robot Report
article_items.description: Festo has released a compact cobot gripper, and its robot-agnostic GripperAI enables grasping of objects without prior training.
article_items.excerpt: Festo has released a compact cobot gripper, and its robot-agnostic GripperAI enables grasping of objects without prior training.
article_items.summary: has launched the HPPH two-finger pneumatic parallel gripper, a lightweight system designed to overcome collaborative robot payload and wiring constraints by integrating the controls, sensing, and certified safety functions directly into the gripper body. The new gripper integrates the pneumatic control valve, position sensors , and electrical interface directly into the gripper body. The software enables robots to grip previously unseen or chaotically positioned objects, including bulk materials, without the need for programming or teach-in training, saving significant setup time, according to the company. The company also shared its progress with the GripperAI software. If higher gripping force is required, the force limit can be disabled to support non-collaborative applications with gripping forces up to approximately 40.5 lbf (180 newtons). article_items.title: The next chapter in flood resilience: Open sourcing Google’s hydrology framework
article_items.excerpt: Explore our many areas of focus Building a collaborative ecosystem Shaping the future together Translating discovery into real-world impact Explore our many areas of focus Building a collaborative ecosystem Shaping the future together Translating discovery into real-world impact The next chapter in flood resilience: Open sourcing Google’s hydrology framework Grey Nearing and Deborah Cohen, Research Scientists, Google Research We have open-sourced our hydrology model to enable National Meteorological and Hydrological Services to integrate advanced AI-based flood forecasting into their own workflows. Floods are one of the most devastating natural hazards worldwide, often arriving with...
article_items.summary: The hydrology modeling package includes model architectures based on Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) Networks, and a training pipeline that allows these models to be trained using historical river data from the open source Caravan dataset . Releasing our model architecture and training pipeline represents a fundamental shift in global flood preparedness, allowing National Meteorological and Hydrological Services (NMHSs), other meteorological agencies, and authorities to retain full control of their data while. It is developed to allow hydrological scientists to build on what we have done at Google Research by adding and testing new models, data, and approaches. CHMI also developed an adapter that integrates the hydrology open source framework into the Delft-FEWS platform, a popular operational flood forecasting tool used by national and local flood forecasting agencies, NGOs, and private companies to drive predictive models. This open source modeling framework allows researchers and forecasters to train AI flood forecasting models with the same architecture and similar training data to what is used to power riverine flood forecasts on Google’s Flood Hub.
article_items.what_happened: The hydrology modeling package includes model architectures based on Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) Networks, and a training pipeline that allows these models to be trained using historical river data from the open source Caravan dataset . Releasing our model architecture and training pipeline represents a fundamental shift in global flood preparedness, allowing National Meteorological and Hydrological Services (NMHSs), other meteorological agencies, and authorities to retain full control of their data while.
article_items.why_matters: It is developed to allow hydrological scientists to build on what we have done at Google Research by adding and testing new models, data, and approaches. CHMI also developed an adapter that integrates the hydrology open source framework into the Delft-FEWS platform, a popular operational flood forecasting tool used by national and local flood forecasting agencies, NGOs, and private companies to drive predictive models.
article_items.what_to_watch: This open source modeling framework allows researchers and forecasters to train AI flood forecasting models with the same architecture and similar training data to what is used to power riverine flood forecasts on Google’s Flood Hub.
key_points: The hydrology modeling package includes model architectures based on Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) Networks, and a training pipeline that allows these models to be trained using historical river data from the open.

Releasing our model architecture and training pipeline represents a fundamental shift in global flood preparedness, allowing National Meteorological and Hydrological Services (NMHSs), other meteorological agencies, and.

This open source modeling framework allows researchers and forecasters to train AI flood forecasting models with the same architecture and similar training data to what is used to power riverine flood forecasts on.

These frameworks are relatively easy and inexpensive to train, providing accuracy without the complexity of traditional hydrological forecasting models and allowing users to incorporate their own specialized data for.
It is developed to allow hydrological scientists to build on what we have done at Google Research by adding and testing new models, data, and approaches.

We at WMO are keen to support open-source, interoperable, Member-driven models and tools that can help save lives and advance the global mission to ensure communities everywhere are forewarned about hazards to protect.

From theory to operational reality In the Global Status of Multi-Hazard Early Warning Systems 2025 report , the World Meteorological Organization recognizes that both local data and Indigenous and Local Knowledge (ILK).

Beyond larger institutions like CHMI, the open source model release offers a scalable, accessible tool, democratizing access to advanced forecasting and opening the door for resource-constrained regions and local teams.
The new model builds upon the foundational success of our initial research by transitioning to a new model architecture.
This allows CHMI and other hydrological services worldwide to use the model in their standard workflows.
Our hydrology model is a Python package that uses the open source PyTorch machine learning modeling package to implement the river forecast model that drives the Google Flood Hub.

This code repository includes two distinct versions of our hydrological model: the original version tested in our benchmarking study published in 2024 , and an upgraded model that currently powers real-time global flood.

rag_rules: Lead with the actual product, model, or API name.
Explain what changed before explaining why it matters.
Anchor credibility with dates, model names, pricing, availability, benchmarks, regions, or partners when the source provides them.
Separate proven capability from claimed capability.
